text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform a bilateral transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) repair for symptomatic bilateral inguinal hernias. The sigmoid colon is visibly protruding into the hernia on the left side. On the right side, a direct inguinal hernia is observed. The prostate appears enlarged, and the bladder is not fully emptied despite the presence of a Foley catheter.
The surgeon creates a large flap across the midline towards the left side, removing the bridge of the umbilical ligaments during the robotic bilateral inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon begins dissection towards the pubic bone, focusing on this area as it is typically the easiest and least risky part of the procedure.

The surgeon begins reducing the direct hernia defect, encountering an extra wad of fat within the area. The transverse fascia is visible, and some fat is left inside the defect, with uncertainty about whether part of the bladder is involved.

The surgeon performs inferior dissection to expose the pubic bone, examining the fat protruding into the hernia. The dissection reveals potential bladder involvement, prompting careful tunneling behind the fat to expose the area.

The surgeon visualizes the pubic bone through the created tunnel, confirming the safety of the dissection. The plane between the transverse fascia is visible, and the surgeon begins to parietalize the cord structures, noting the absence of an indirect defect.

The surgeon identifies and amputates a confusing mass of fat, initially thought to be part of the retroperitoneal lymph node packet, while continuing the parietalization and lateralization of the cord structures.
The surgeon encounters a large amount of fat protruding into the indirect inguinal ring, identified as a large lipoma, and begins the careful process of reducing it.
The surgeon attempts to reduce a large lipoma in a bloodless fashion, but bleeding occurs. After successfully removing the lipoma, the surgeon prepares to reposition it.

The surgeon inadvertently dissects behind a retroperitoneal lymph node, where the lipoma tissue is confluent with the lymph node packet. The surgeon identifies the lymph node and the lipoma, attempting to return to the proper dissection plane to separate these structures.
The surgeon encounters increased bleeding due to dissection in the wrong plane, revealing a lymph node. The surgeon tucks behind the cord structures and continues to retract the lipoma.

The surgeon carefully performs focused cautery and blunt dissection around the hernia sac, which intermittently moves in and out of view. The dissection is performed with caution due to the proximity of the sigmoid colon, which forms part of the hernia wall.

The surgeon searches for the cord structures while dissecting down to the peritoneum, removing fat from its surface. The large hernia presents challenges with retraction, and a hole inadvertently made in the peritoneum is visible, which will be repaired later with flap closure.
The surgeon sweeps the cord structures laterally to expose the hernia sac. The hernia sac is visible, and the surgeon ensures the sigmoid colon is not involved before proceeding with the dissection.

The surgeon continues to follow the hernia defect, identifying the peritoneal hole created earlier. The view shifts laterally to facilitate the removal of the hernia sac. The sigmoid colon has been confirmed to be reduced, allowing the surgeon to separate the vas and cord structures from the hernia sac and prepare for its amputation.

The surgeon clears fat from the hernia sac to reveal the glistening white surface, identifying the vas deferens. The surgeon struggles to maintain traction, indicating the potential benefit of an assist or a fourth robotic arm.

The surgeon uses sweeping motions to separate the cord structures and the vas deferens from the hernia sac, creating a visible window inferior to the sac. The vas deferens and the inferior portion of the hernia sac are clearly identified.

The surgeon carefully widens the window between the hernia sac and the cord structures, ensuring the gonadal vessels and vas are separated. The preserved cord structures are visible laterally, with the vas positioned medially, allowing for safe division without risk of transecting these structures. Parietalization is performed to further secure the area.

The surgical field appears slightly bloodier than anticipated, prompting the surgeon to place a sponge in the abdominal cavity to absorb excess blood. Fatty tissue is visible entering the canal, separate from the cord structures, and is being carefully peeled off the top of the cord. The surgeon continues dissection to differentiate between lipoma and retroperitoneal nodal tissue.

The surgeon identifies the iliac vein and decides to stop pursuing it further, opting to amputate the lipoma where it adheres to lymph node tissue. The nodal tissue is repositioned behind the cord structures, completing the parietalization to prepare for mesh placement.
The surgeon places a twelve by sixteen anatomic mesh on the left side, followed by a similar mesh on the right side, ensuring that the meshes overlap at the midline to cover the bilateral MPOs.

The surgeon is satisfied with the placement of the mesh and proceeds to suture the flap closed. The closure begins in the middle, incorporating the large gap left from the amputation of the sac on the left side. The redundant peritoneum allows for a straightforward closure.
The surgeon cinches the suture tightly to completely close the hole, then proceeds to finish suturing the flap.

The surgeon sutures the two meshes together at the midline, using extra suture to secure them. A point of fixation is applied to prevent mesh eventration into the defect. The direct defect is small, so inversion or closure is deemed unnecessary. The surgeon proceeds to close the right side.
The surgeon closes a remaining hole in the surgical field, completing the procedure.
